prompt: We're building an AI-powered legal document review platform. We use LLMs to analyze contracts, flag risks, extract key terms, and compare against playbooks. Seed stage, $4M raised. Target: legal operations teams and in-house counsel at companies with 10+ lawyers. Should feel authoritative and precise, but NOT stuffy Latin (no Lexicon, no Juris, no Veritas - those are played out). Think more Clio/Relativity energy. This is a regulated industry - can't use words like 'attorney', 'counsel', 'legal advice'. Competitors: Kira Systems, eBrevia, Luminance, Ironclad, ContractPodAi, Harvey. The name should signal 'built for lawyers' without sounding like a law firm. Needs to work internationally - UK and EU markets from day one.

assertions.text: At least 6 of 11 naming categories attempted
Vertical Code Calibration applied for legaltech
Regulated-Industry Lexical Compliance applied (avoided 'attorney', 'counsel', 'legal advice')
Avoided transparent Latin clichés per user constraint AND skill guidance (Lex-, Juris-, Veritas-)
Buyer Committee test applied
Sales Conversation Fit templates tested
Enterprise Objection Preemption applied
Cross-language checks applied (UK + EU markets)
Ownability-first compounds generated in parallel track
Classical Stem Engineering applied (but naturalized, not transparent Latin per cliché guard)
Personified Names explored (Harvey is a competitor - this angle is proven in legaltech)
Lexicon Three-Team Method applied
Each riff labeled with which technique produced it
10+ riffs per direction
Homonym/Synonym/Sound-Alike Riffing applied
B2B riffing checklist used
Hard gate checks displayed before generation menu
Inline conflict flagging with warning emoji
At least 40 unique candidates generated
Category Creation Naming considered (is this joining or creating a category?)
Name Lifecycle Stress Test considered (what if they expand beyond doc review?)
